intro: The sine and cosine rules let you work with any triangle — not just right-angled ones. Use them when you know some sides and angles but need to find others that you cannot reach using basic trigonometry.
explanation:
Label a triangle so that side a is opposite angle A, side b is opposite angle B, and side c is opposite angle C.

Sine Rule:
a / sin A = b / sin B = c / sin C

Use the sine rule when you know:
- Two angles and one side (AAS or ASA), OR
- Two sides and an angle opposite one of them (SSA).

To find a side: a = b x sin A / sin B.
To find an angle: sin A = a x sin B / b.

Cosine Rule:
a squared = b squared + c squared - 2bc cos A

Rearranged to find angle A:
cos A = (b squared + c squared - a squared) / (2bc)

Use the cosine rule when you know:
- Two sides and the included angle (SAS), OR
- All three sides (SSS).

Area of any triangle:
Area = 1/2 x a x b x sin C
where C is the angle between sides a and b.

Deciding which rule to use:
- Have angle and opposite side pair available? Use sine rule.
- Have two sides and included angle, or all three sides? Use cosine rule.

worked_example.question: In triangle ABC, a = 7 cm, b = 9 cm, and angle C = 64 degrees. Find side c and the area of the triangle.
steps: Use the cosine rule: c squared = a squared + b squared - 2ab cos C.
c squared = 7 squared + 9 squared - 2 x 7 x 9 x cos 64.
c = square root of 74.76 = 8.65 cm (3 s.f.).
Area = 1/2 x 7 x 9 x sin 64 = 1/2 x 63 x 0.8988 = 28.3 cm squared (3 s.f.).

common_mistakes: Using the sine rule when you have two sides and the included angle — in that case you must use the cosine rule.
Forgetting to square root at the end of the cosine rule — c squared = ... gives c squared, not c.
Using degrees in calculations when the calculator is in radian mode — always check your calculator is set to degrees for GCSE questions.
